source_references.title: Ancient Maori Myths, Legends and Tales
source_references.author: A.W. Reed

full_text: Across the dark water of Lake Rotorua, on the island of Mokoia, Tutanekai played his flute each night. The notes floated over the water like silver birds, and on the shore, Hinemoa listened. She was a high-born maiden, beautiful and proud, and her family had chosen a husband for her — a man of wealth and rank. But Hinemoa's heart belonged to the music. Tutanekai was a younger son, not wealthy, not powerful, but his music was the most beautiful sound in the world. He played for her across the water, and she knew that she loved him. One night, she could bear it no longer. She tied gourds to her body for buoyancy — three large gourds, one for each arm and one for her waist — and she walked into the cold water. The lake was deep and dark, and the night was moonless. She swam. The water was freezing, and her arms ached, and the gourds bumped against her body with each stroke. But she did not stop. She followed the music. When she reached Mokoia Island, she pulled herself onto the shore, dripping and shivering, and followed the sound of the flute to where Tutanekai sat. He saw her and could not speak. She saw him and could not weep. They stood together in the firelight, and the music stopped, for the song was complete. When her family learned what had happened, they relented. No one could deny a love that had crossed a lake in darkness. The Maori tell this story as the greatest love legend of their people — a testament to the power of music, the courage of the heart, and the truth that love, once found, will not be denied.

title: Hi'iaka's Journey to Bring Lohiau to Pele
summary: Pele fell in love with Lohiau, a chief of Kauai, during a spirit dream. She sent her beloved youngest sister Hi'iaka to retrieve him. Hi'iaka agreed on the condition that Pele protect her sacred lehua groves and friend Hopoe. On Kauai, Hi'iaka found Lohiau had died of heartbreak and spent thirty days chanting to revive him. On the journey back, Pele grew jealous of Hi'iaka's prolonged absence. In rage, Pele destroyed Hi'iaka's lehua groves and encased Hopoe in lava. Hi'iaka, seeing this through far-sight, defiantly embraced Lohiau at the crater's edge. Pele engulfed them in flames. Hi'iaka revived Lohiau again, and ultimately Pele allowed them to return to Kauai together.

source_references.title: Legends of Gods, Ghosts, and Goblins
source_references.author: Laura S. Green

source_references.title: Pele and Hi'iaka: A Myth from Hawai'i
source_references.author: Natalie M. Gilbert

full_text: Pele, the goddess of fire and volcanoes, dreamed of Lohiau — a chief of Kauai whose beauty had reached even the goddess's burning heart. She sent her youngest and most beloved sister, Hi'iaka, to bring him to her. Hi'iaka agreed, but on one condition: Pele must protect her sacred lehua groves and her dear friend Hopoe while she was gone. Pele promised, and Hi'iaka set out across the sea. On Kauai, she found Lohiau dead of heartbreak — for he too had dreamed of her, and the waiting had killed him. Hi'iaka spent thirty days chanting over his body, singing the songs of life until his breath returned and his eyes opened. Then she began the journey back to Hawaii, with Lohiau at her side. But the journey took longer than Pele expected. Jealousy, the most dangerous of all fires, ignited in the goddess's heart. She believed Hi'iaka had forgotten her, that Lohiau was too beautiful to surrender, that betrayal walked beside them on the road. In rage, Pele destroyed Hi'iaka's lehua groves, burning the sacred trees to ash. She encased Hopoe in lava, freezing her friend in stone. When Hi'iaka saw this through her far-sight, her grief and fury were beyond words. She embraced Lohiau at the crater's edge, defying her sister. Pele engulfed them in flames, but Hi'iaka had the power to revive the dead. She brought Lohiau back again, and at last Pele relented. She allowed them to return to Kauai together, bound by a love that had survived fire, death, and the jealousy of a goddess.

title: Hikule'o, Tangaloa, and Maui Divide the Cosmos
summary: In Tongan creation myth, after the primordial god Taufulifonua fathered Hikule'o with his sister Havea Lolofonua, the three great powers divided the universe. Hikule'o received Pulotu, the underworld realm of spirits and the afterlife. Tangaloa took the vast sky and heavens, becoming ruler of the celestial domain. Maui received the underworld (the earth's foundation) and was tasked with fishing up the islands from the ocean. This tripartite division established the cosmic order—the sky above (Tangaloa's realm), the earth's surface (under Maui's influence), and the underworld of Pulotu (Hikule'o's domain where souls journey after death). The balance between these three realms governs Tongan spiritual beliefs.
full_text: In the beginning, before the world was divided, the three great powers of Tonga shared a single realm. Hikule'o, Tangaloa, and Maui were sons of the primordial god, each carrying a different aspect of creation's weight. But three gods cannot share one world without conflict, so a council was held. The division was drawn with the precision of a blade. Hikule'o received Pulotu — the underworld, the realm of spirits and the afterlife. It is a place of shadows and memory, where the dead walk the paths that the living have forgotten. Tangaloa took the sky — the vast, open heavens where the stars burn and the wind roars. He became the ruler of the celestial domain, his eyes on the horizon, his hands in the clouds. Maui received the most dangerous of the three: the earth's foundation, the realm beneath the surface where the roots of mountains reach down into darkness. From this realm, Maui would fish the islands up from the ocean — pulling the land itself from the deep with his magical hook. This tripartite division established the cosmic order that governs Tongan spiritual belief. The sky above is Tangaloa's realm, where prayers ascend. The earth's surface is Maui's domain, where humans live and work. And below, in the darkness of Pulotu, Hikule'o waits with the dead. The balance between these three realms is the balance of the world itself. When one is disturbed, the others feel it — in storms, in earthquakes, in the restless spirits that walk the earth on moonless nights. The Tongans say that the division was just, for each god received what he was strongest enough to hold.

title: Hiro the Trickster and the Magic Canoe
summary: Hiro, the Tahitian trickster god of thieves and the son of the god Ta'aroa, built a magical canoe that could sail faster than the wind. Using his cunning and his enchanted vessel, Hiro traveled across the Society Islands performing daring thefts and outsmarting chiefs and gods alike. He stole fish from the markets, food from the temples, and once even attempted to steal the island of Bora Bora itself. His exploits angered the other gods, who eventually unleashed a great storm to stop him. The story of Hiro's rebellion and eventual downfall teaches that even the cleverest trickster cannot escape divine justice, but his resourcefulness remains celebrated in Tahitian storytelling.
full_text: Hiro was the son of Ta'aroa, the supreme god, and he had inherited none of his father's seriousness. Where Ta'aroa spoke in thunder, Hiro whispered in laughter. Where Ta'aroa created, Hiro took. He was the god of thieves, and he gloried in it. He built a canoe that defied the laws of the sea — a vessel that sailed faster than the wind, that turned without tacking, that vanished in mist when pursued. With this magic canoe, Hiro traveled the Society Islands, stealing with a impunity that enraged the other gods. He stole fish from the temple markets of Tahiti, where they had been offered to the spirits. He stole food from the kitchens of chiefs, eating it on the shore while the cooks searched the villages. He stole water from sacred springs, pouring it into the ocean as if it were worthless. His most audacious theft was the island of Bora Bora itself — or so he claimed. He tried to tow it behind his canoe, but the island was too heavy even for magic, and it stayed where it was, forever beyond the reach of a trickster's greed. The other gods grew tired of Hiro's deploys. They sent storms to capsize his canoe, waves to swamp it, and winds to tear its sail. Hiro outran them all. At last, the gods caught him through a trick of their own — a calm so complete that even magic could not sail through it. Hiro was stranded, his canoe beached, his laughter silenced. He was not destroyed — the gods do not destroy their own — but he was humbled. The Tahitians tell Hiro's stories with a mixture of admiration and disapproval, for Hiro represents the part of every person that wants to take what is not given. His resourcefulness is celebrated; his greed is cautioned against.

title: Hotu Matu'a Discovers Rapa Nui
summary: King Hotu Matu'a lived in the mythical land of Hiva, which was sinking beneath the ocean. A prophet named Hau Maka appeared in Hotu Matu'a's dream and told him to search for a new island called Rapa Nui. Seven explorers were sent ahead in a canoe, finding the uninhabited island. They returned to report its location. Hotu Matu'a then sailed with two large canoes carrying approximately a hundred people, including his wife, children, and essential plants and animals. When they landed at Anakena Beach, Hotu Matu'a established the first settlement and divided the island among his descendants. His nine sons became the ancestors of the Rapa Nui clans, and he inaugurated the moai carving tradition to honor his lineage.

source_references.title: The Rapanui (Easter Islanders): Prehistoric Culture in Polynesia
source_references.author: J.J. Brémond

source_references.title: Aku-Aku: The Secret of Easter Island
source_references.author: Thor Heyerdahl

full_text: In the mythical land of Hiva, the ocean was rising. King Hotu Matu'a watched the water creep higher each season, swallowing gardens and villages, turning fields to salt marsh. The elders whispered that Hiva was sinking, that the gods had turned their backs on the land. One night, a prophet named Hau Maka appeared in Hotu Matu'a's dream. He showed the king a vision of a new island — green, fertile, surrounded by clear water. 'This is Rapa Nui,' Hau Maka said. 'Go there. Take your people. Build a new world.' Hotu Matu'a sent seven explorers ahead in a canoe. They sailed east for many days, following the stars and the birds, and at last they found the island — uninhabited, lush, and waiting. They carved a stone with the king's mark and planted it on the shore, then returned to Hiva with the news. Hotu Matu'a ordered two great canoes to be built. They loaded them with a hundred people — his wife, his children, his counselors — and every plant and animal that could be carried. Breadfruit, banana, sugarcane, chickens, dogs. They sailed into the eastern sea, and after many days of wind and wave, they landed at Anakena Beach, where the sand is white and the palms sway in the gentle wind. Hotu Matu'a walked ashore and planted his staff in the sand. 'This is home,' he said. He divided the island among his nine sons, each becoming the ancestor of a clan. And he inaugurated the moai — the great stone figures that would honor his lineage and watch over his people for a thousand years.

title: How the Moai Walked
summary: According to Rapanui oral tradition, the great stone moai statues were carved at the Rano Raraku quarry and then walked to their final ahu (ceremonial platforms) across the island. The moai walked at night, powered by mana from the ancestors channeled through the kahuna (priests). Using a system of ropes and rocking motions, teams would chant and coordinate to make the massive statues 'walk' upright, step by step, sometimes for miles. The largest moai, Paro, walked over 10 kilometers before taking its position at Ahu Te Pito Kura. One legend describes how a moai stopped mid-journey when the villagers broke a sacred tapu, and it remains fallen to this day, frozen by the broken taboo.

source_references.title: The Easter Island Statue: Style and Technique in Their Chronological Sequence
source_references.author: John P. Jennings and Christian E.岛

full_text: The moai were not dragged to their platforms. They walked. This is what the Rapanui say, and they say it with certainty, as one states a fact of daily life. At the quarry of Rano Raraku, the moai were carved from the volcanic tuff — their faces rising from the stone, their hands at their sides, their eyes looking inward toward the mountain. When a moai was complete, it was freed from the rock and stood upright, waiting. Then, under the direction of the kahuna — the priests — teams of men gathered around the statue. Ropes were tied around its head and base. The men chanted, their voices rising and falling in rhythm, and they pulled the moai forward in a rocking motion — left, right, left, right — each swing carrying the statue a step closer to its final resting place. The moai walked at night, for the mana of the ancestors was strongest in darkness. Under the stars, the statues swayed across the landscape, their stone bodies creaking with each step, their eyes — when the coral eyes were placed — gleaming in the torchlight. The largest moai, Paro, walked over ten kilometers from the quarry to Ahu Te Pito Kura, the greatest of the ceremonial platforms. Not every moai reached its destination. One legend tells of a moai that stopped mid-journey when the villagers broke a sacred tapu — a prohibition that had been set by the priests. The statue froze, lost its mana, and fell. It lies where it fell to this day, its face in the dirt, a testament to the power of broken law. The walking moai are proof that the Rapanui understood something the modern world sometimes forgets: that great things are accomplished not by force but by faith, coordination, and the shared belief that stone can walk if the spirit moves it.

title: Hina: The Woman in the Moon
summary: Hina (also called Sina or Ina across different Polynesian cultures) was a mortal woman who became the moon goddess. In the Maori version, Hina went to fetch water at night and cursed the moon when she stumbled in darkness. The moon descended, caught her, and lifted her into the sky. In Hawaiian tradition, Hina was the mother of Maui who escaped human troubles and found refuge in the moon. Cook Islands stories tell that the moon god Marama fell in love with Ina and carried her away. In Samoa, Hina makes tapa cloth in the moon, her form visible during full moon nights. Across Polynesia, Hina represents the divine feminine, the cycle of death and rebirth, and the celestial light that guides voyagers at night.

source_references.title: Legends of Maui: A Demigod of Polynesia
source_references.author: S. Percy Smith

full_text: In the earliest days, when the gods still walked among mortals, there lived a woman called Hina—known also as Sina or Ina in the far-flung islands of the Pacific—who was beautiful beyond measure yet restless in the world of men. In the Maori telling, Hina went to draw water at night, for the burden of daylight chores left her no peace. She stumbled on the dark path and cursed the moon for its feeble light, and the moon, hearing her voice and moved by her sorrow, descended from the heavens and caught her in its pale arms. It lifted her upward through the clouds until she stood upon its luminous surface, where she would shine forever. In the Hawaiian tradition the tale takes another cast: Hina was mother to the great demigod Maui, and the troubles of mortal life grew too heavy for her. She fled to the moon to find sanctuary, and on full nights her son would look up and see her form—sometimes as a woman pounding tapa cloth, sometimes as a shadow cast across the silver disc. The Cook Islands tell that the moon god Marama fell in love with Ina and carried her away to dwell with him among the stars. In Samoa, Hina is the weaver of tapa cloth in the moon, her rhythmic beating a sound the faithful claim they can still hear when the night is still. Across all the islands of Polynesia, Hina is the face of the divine feminine—the cycle of death and rebirth made visible, the celestial light that guides voyagers across the trackless sea, and the reminder that even the darkest night holds a promise of return.

title: Hatuibwari: The Dragon God of the Solomon Islands
summary: Hatuibwari is a powerful dragon-like being from the mythology of the Solomon Islands, particularly among the people of Guadalcanal. Described as a massive serpent with the features of a dragon, Hatuibwari is a creator and cultural hero who shaped the islands and taught humans essential skills. In some accounts, Hatuibwari is a dual-sex being who created the first humans from clay and gave them fire, tools, and knowledge of agriculture. The being is associated with both creation and destruction, capable of bringing rain and fertility but also earthquakes and storms when angered. Hatuibwari's serpentine nature connects it to the widespread Melanesian motif of powerful snake deities who play central roles in creation myths across the region.

source_references.title: The Melanesians: Studies in their Anthropology and Folk-Lore
source_references.author: R.R. Marett

full_text: Long before the islands of Guadalcanal took their present form, the great dragon-being Hatuibwari moved across the primordial waters, carving land from the sea with the sweep of its serpentine tail. Hatuibwari was no ordinary creature but a being of tremendous power—described in the oldest oral traditions as a colossal serpent bearing the features of a dragon, whose scales caught the light of the first sunrise and whose breath carried the scent of rain and fertile earth. The people of the Solomon Islands speak of Hatuibwari as both creator and culture hero, for the being shaped the islands and then taught the first humans the arts of survival. From clay and red earth, Hatuibwari formed the earliest men and women, breathing fire into their nostrils so they might cook, and pressing knowledge of agriculture into their minds so they might sow taro and yam. In some accounts Hatuibwari possessed a dual nature—both male and female—embodying the complete creative force from which all life proceeds. When pleased, Hatuibwari brought generous rain and calm seas that swelled the harvests; when angered by human vanity or neglect of proper offerings, the dragon-god sent earthquakes splitting the hillsides and storms that tore the canoes from their moorings. The serpentine form of Hatuibwari connects the Solomon Islands to the wider Melanesian tradition of great snake deities who stand at the center of creation, their coils wrapped around the roots of the world, their hissing the sound of thunder rolling down from the mountain ridges.

title: The Hintha Bird Legend
summary: The Hintha bird — a mythical golden goose or Brahminy duck — is the sacred emblem of the Mon people and the symbol of Bago (Pegu) Region in Myanmar. According to Mon Buddhist legend, the Hintha bird was the Bodhisatta (a previous incarnation of the Buddha) in the form of a wise golden goose. In the Jataka tale of the Hintha Bird, a pair of Hintha birds lived on a golden lake in the Himalayas. The king of the birds taught the creatures of the lake important moral lessons about non-attachment and the dangers of pride. The Hintha tradition also tells the story of how the Mon people, guided by a pair of Hintha birds, found their homeland. Two birds — male and female — led the Mon king and his people across the sea to the shores of southern Burma, where they established the kingdom of Suvannabhumi (Golden Land). The two Hintha birds, forever together, became symbols of Mon identity, representing fidelity, prosperity, and the golden purity of the Buddhist faith. Their paired image adorns Mon state flags and Bago's official emblem.
moral: Wisdom and moral purity, like the golden Hintha, illuminate the path to a promised homeland.
source: Mon Buddhist tradition, Jataka tales, Bago region folklore
full_text: The golden lake lay in the Himalayas, its surface unbroken, its waters holding the reflection of the sky like a mirror holds light. Upon its shores, the Hintha birds gathered — golden geese of extraordinary beauty, their feathers catching the sun and scattering it in all directions. Among them was the Bodhisatta, the future Buddha, who had taken the form of a wise golden goose and lived among the birds, teaching them the dharma in the language of flight and silence. The king of the Hintha birds was a stern ruler, and the creatures of the lake feared his judgments. But the Bodhisatta-goose taught differently. He taught through stories — tales of generosity and cruelty, of the dangers of pride and the rewards of humility. The creatures listened, and the lake became a place of wisdom. In another telling, the Hintha birds are the guides of the Mon people. When the Mon king and his followers were driven from their homeland, they prayed for a sign. Two birds — male and female, inseparable as the morning and its light — appeared and led them across the sea to the shores of southern Burma, to the land they would call Suvannabhumi, the Golden Land. The birds led them to the banks of the Bago River, where they founded the kingdom of Pegu. The paired Hintha birds became the symbol of Mon identity — faithful, prosperous, and golden as the Buddhist faith they carried with them. Their image adorns the flags of the Mon people and the official emblem of the Bago Region. They are not merely birds. They are the memory of a migration, the promise of a homeland, and the living emblem of a people who have endured centuries of conquest and still carry their faith like a flame in cupped hands.

title: The Hmong Creation Myth: The Flood and the Siblings
summary: The Hmong creation myth centers on a great flood that destroyed the world and the surviving sibling pair who repopulated it. According to the oral tradition passed down through generations, a great argument between the Sky Father (Saub) and the Earth led to a devastating flood that covered the entire world. Only two siblings — usually named as a brother and sister, known by various names in different clans — survived by taking refuge inside a large wooden funeral drum or a hollow gourd that floated on the waters. After the flood receded, they found themselves alone at the peak of a mountain. The brother proposed marriage to continue the human race, but the sister refused, believing it was wrong. After consulting with the spirit world, they were told to roll two millstones down opposite sides of the mountain; if the stones met at the bottom, they were meant to marry. The stones rolled and met, and the siblings married. The sister gave birth to a strange gourd-like egg or mass, which they cut open, releasing all the different peoples of the earth — including the Hmong, Chinese, and Lao. This legend explains Hmong ethnic identity and their relationship to neighboring peoples, while establishing the sacred bond between siblings and the role of divine guidance in human affairs.
moral: Divine will can transcend human conventions when the survival of all humanity is at stake.
source: Hmong oral tradition, Hmong shamanic texts, oral epics
full_text: The Sky Father was angry. Saub, the great deity who governed the heavens, looked down upon the earth and saw corruption — people lying, cheating, breaking the taboos that held the world together. He sent the rain. It fell for forty days and forty nights, or perhaps longer, for time moves differently when the world is ending. The rivers rose. The mountains drowned. The villages vanished beneath brown water that carried away the houses, the fields, the graves of the ancestors. All of it — gone. But not all. Two siblings survived, a brother and a sister, who had taken refuge inside a great wooden funeral drum that floated upon the flood. They were young, they were terrified, and they were alone. When the waters receded, they found themselves on a mountaintop, surrounded by mud and silence. The world was empty. They had to continue the human race, and there was only one way to do it. The brother proposed marriage. The sister refused. It was wrong, she said — siblings should not share a bed. They consulted the spirits, and the spirits gave them a test: roll two millstones down opposite sides of the mountain. If the stones meet at the bottom, you are meant to marry. The stones rolled. They curved. They met. The sister accepted. They married, and from their union came a strange birth — not a child but a gourd, an egg-like mass of flesh. They cut it open, and from within poured the peoples of the earth: the Hmong, the Chinese, the Lao, the Thai, each emerging from a different piece of the gourd. This legend explains the Hmong relationship to their neighbors — they are siblings, born from the same origin, differentiated by the blade that divided them. It also establishes the sacred role of the shaman, who mediates between the Sky Father's anger and the earth's suffering, keeping the covenant that the siblings renewed when the flood receded and the world began again.

title: Heer Ranjha — The Tragic Lovers of Punjab
summary: Heer, the beautiful daughter of a wealthy Jatt family in Jhang, fell deeply in love with Ranjha, a youngest son who tended cattle and played the flute. They met secretly for years until Heer's jealous uncle Kaido exposed them. Heer's family forcibly married her to another man. The heartbroken Ranjha became a wandering Jogi (ascetic), piercing his ears and renouncing worldly life. Traveling across Punjab, he eventually found Heer again. Her parents finally agreed to their union, but on the wedding day, Heer's uncle poisoned her. Ranjha, seeing her die, took the same poison and died beside her. The lovers are buried together in Jhang, where their tomb remains a pilgrimage site. Waris Shah's 1766 poetic rendition elevated this folk tragedy into the quintessential Punjabi romance.
source: Heer by Waris Shah (1766), Punjabi oral tradition
moral: Love transcends death but society's cruelty destroys it

source_references.title: Heer Ranjha: A Study of the Punjabi Classic
source_references.author: Shamim Hanfi (ed.)
source_references.note: Critical study of the Heer-Ranjha qissa tradition in Punjabi literature, analyzing Waris Shah's 1766 version and its roots in oral Sufi folk romance.

full_text: In the wheat fields of Jhang, where the wind moves through the grain like a whispered secret, Heer Ranjha once lived and loved and died. Heer was the daughter of a wealthy Jatt family, a girl of such beauty that the poets of Punjab say the jasmine bowed its head when she walked past. Ranjha was the youngest of four brothers, a cattle-herder who played the tumbi — a single-stringed instrument whose note is as lonely as the Thar wind. They met at a fair, where Ranjha played and Heer listened, and the wheat fields around them swayed in a wind that seemed to know what was happening. For years they met in secret, in the orchards and along the riverbanks, until Heer's jealous uncle Kaido discovered them and told the family. Heer was married off to a man she did not love, and Ranjha, heartbroken, became a Jogi — an ascetic who wanders the world with nothing, his ears pierced, his hair matted, his heart a country without borders. He walked across Punjab, sleeping in dargahs and under trees, playing his tumbi for anyone who would listen. Eventually he found his way back to Jhang, and Heer saw him again across a crowded courtyard. Her parents, moved by Ranjha's wandering and Heer's unhappiness, agreed to let them marry. But on the wedding day, Heer's uncle Kaido poisoned her sweets. Ranjha found her dying and, without hesitation, took the poison himself. They were buried side by side in Jhang, and their tomb is still visited by lovers who tie threads to the iron grille and pray for a love that lasts beyond death. Waris Shah, who set their story to verse in 1766, called it the Romeo and Juliet of the East, and the comparison is apt — but in Punjab, they say Heer and Ranjha loved each other first.

title: How Anansi Got His Stories
summary: Long ago, all stories in the forest belonged to Tiger, and were called Tiger Stories. Anansi, tired of being mocked as the weakest creature, demanded that stories be named after him. Tiger set an impossible task: capture Snake. After failing with a noose, a pit trap, and a cage, Anansi tricked Snake by challenging his length against bamboo. Anansi tied Snake's head and tail to the bamboo, proving that brains can overcome brawn. From that day, all stories became Anansi Stories. The phrase "Jack Mandora, mi nuh choose none" is traditionally said at the end to absolve the storyteller of Anansi's mischievous deeds.
source: Martha Warren Beckwith, Jamaica Anansi Stories (1924); Real Jamaica Vacations
moral: Brains can overcome brawn

Tiger set an impossible task: capture Snake. After failing with a noose, a pit trap, and a cage, Anansi tricked Snake by challenging his length against bamboo. title: Hatim al-Tai: The Legend of Generosity
summary: Hatim ibn Abdallah of the Tai tribe, a pre-Islamic Arab chief in 6th-century Yemen, became the enduring symbol of Arab generosity and chivalry. Countless legends recount his extreme hospitality: slaughtering his prized horse when no other food was available for guests, giving away his possessions until left with nothing, and offering his own life to an assassin sent by a jealous king rather than sending the man back empty-handed. Even after death, legend says he hosted travelers from beyond the grave. The name 'Hatim' remains synonymous with generosity across the Arab and Persian world, and his son Adi became a companion of the Prophet Muhammad.

source_references.title: Medieval Arab Historians and the Fall of Civilisation
source_references.author: Amikam Elad

source_references.title: The Encyclopaedia of Islam
source_references.author: C.E. Bosworth, E. van Donzel, W.P. Heinrichs, G. Lecomte

full_text:
There was a man in the old Arabia whose name became the very word for generosity, and that man was Hatim ibn Abdallah of the tribe of Tai, who lived in the sixth century in the highlands of what is now Yemen, and who gave away everything he had and then gave away more than he had, and never once regretted it.

The stories are many and they all follow the same pattern: someone arrives at Hatim's door in need, and Hatim gives. He gives food when his larder is empty, he gives clothes when he has nothing left but the wrap upon his shoulders, he gives his horse when a guest arrives on foot and the journey ahead is long. The most famous tale tells of a time when guests arrived at his camp and there was nothing to eat -- his flocks had been scattered, his stores were bare, his wife had nothing to prepare. Without hesitation, Hatim went out and slaughtered his finest horse, the stallion that had carried him through a hundred raids and whose speed was legendary across the desert. He roasted the meat himself and served it to his guests, and when they had eaten their fill he sat with them and poured coffee and told stories, as if he had not just destroyed the most valuable thing he owned.

But the tale that surpasses all others is this: a king, jealous of Hatim's reputation, sent an assassin to kill him. The assassin found Hatim alone in his camp and revealed his mission. Hatim listened, then said, 'You have come a long way and you have nothing to show for your journey. Take these goods and give them to your king as a gift, and tell him that Hatim sends them with his compliments.' The assassin took the goods and returned to the king, who was so ashamed of his petty jealousy that he sent the goods back and sued for peace.

Hatim died and was buried in the desert, and even after death the legends say he continued to give. Travelers who passed his tomb at night reported seeing a light within the stone and hearing the clatter of plates and the murmur of conversation, as if Hatim were hosting a feast for the spirits of the road. Whether this was true or merely the wish of people who wanted the world to be a kinder place than it is, no one can say. What is certain is that the name Hatim al-Tai has been spoken with reverence across the Arab and Persian world for fourteen centuries, and that in a culture which prizes hospitality above almost all other virtues, there is no higher compliment than to be called a Hatim.

title: Hushang Discovers Fire (Sadeh Feast)
summary: The second king of the Pishdadian dynasty in Persian myth, Hushang, grandson of the first man Gayomard, one day hurls his flint axe at a serpent blocking his path. He misses the snake but the axe strikes a rock, producing a spark. Curious, the king strikes two flints together and creates fire deliberately — the first controlled fire in human history. He proclaims it a divine gift, makes fire his altar, and establishes the Sadeh feast to celebrate the discovery. Hushang also teaches humanity metalworking (fashioning axes, saws, adzes), irrigation, agriculture, animal husbandry, and fur clothing, fundamentally transforming human civilization.

source_references.title: Shahnameh: The Persian Book of Kings
source_references.author: Dick Davis (trans.)

full_text:
Hushang was the grandson of Gayomard, the first man, and he walked the earth in the earliest days of the Pishdadian dynasty, when the world was young and raw and the creatures of the wild still remembered the time before the sky was separated from the ground. He was a king, but kingship in those days meant something different from what it would later become -- it meant responsibility, not privilege, and Hushang bore that responsibility with the seriousness of a man who knows that every action he takes will be remembered for ten thousand years.

One day, walking through the hills with his axe of flint strapped to his waist, Hushang came upon a serpent coiled in the path, its hood spread, its eyes bright with malice. He drew his axe and hurled it at the creature, but the throw went wide, and the axe struck a rock with such force that a spark flew from the impact -- a tiny, brilliant point of orange light that danced on the stone for a moment and then vanished.

Hushang stared at the place where the spark had been. He picked up his axe and struck the rock again, and another spark appeared, and this time he was ready. He gathered dry grass and struck the flints together, and the sparks caught, and a thin curl of smoke rose from the grass, and then a flame, and then a fire -- the first fire that a human being had ever made by his own will.

Hushang stood before the fire and felt its heat upon his face, and he understood that this was a gift from the gods, perhaps from Ahura Mazda himself, the lord of wisdom and light. He knelt and gave thanks, and then he showed his people how to make fire, how to tend it, how to carry it from place to place. He taught them to smelt copper and tin and make bronze, to forge axes and saws and adzes, to dig irrigation channels and direct the water to their fields, to herd cattle and shear sheep and wear the pelts of animals for warmth.

And in remembrance of that first spark, Hushang established the feast of Sadeh, which is celebrated on the hundredth day after the winter solstice, when the fire on the hilltop is lit and the people gather around it and sing and feast and remember that all of human civilization began with a single, accidental strike of flint upon stone.
